title: HyperThings I
description: This is a fantastic set of externals by HyperThings. It is the first of many collections of externals that will be released here in America Online's Mac HyperCard forum. Some of the most fun and useful externals are described below: ArcProgress: Will display color pies and arcs on the screen, allowing you to make *fast* pie-type progress screens. DoAlert 2.0: Will display color dialogs with multiple buttons, color picts and icons, even on-the-fly text, all in one simple line of code! GetPixCol: Get the color of a pixel *anywhere* on the screen. GetRGBColor: Will let you use Apple's standard color picker dialog to get a precise RGB value! MoveRect: Smoothly scroll any rect in the hypercard window! RefreshRect: Update (also known as 'refresh') any rect in the hypercard window! SetCCurs: Now you can set the cursor to a color crsr resource! SimplePop: Lets you make on-the-fly popup menus with ICONs, CICN's, multiple styles, commmand chars, checkmarks, disabled items, and divider lines! This collection has 15 different externals, and the stack is unlocked so you can examine all of it's scripts. It's a great collection of shareware externals that any HyperCard user will find irresistible! Please note: There has been a change of address for HyperThings. If you would like the new address, please send an email to UFOfreak5@aol.com and request the new address. Thank you. STAFF NOTE: Interesting set. Checked for Viruses with Disinfectant & Merry Xmas Vaccine.

title: PlayMovie 4.0 XCMD (Director ...
description: An external allowing you to play a Director 4.0 movie from within HyperCard. This external is not officially supported by MacroMedia. I was told by MM that an engineer had donated his time to write the command. The limited documentation says to refer to the Player 3.0 stack that shipped with Director 3.0 as the documentation for this stack. But there are major differences between the way this XCMD works now and the way it did in the past. The old version supported the movieShowMenu and movieHideMenu parameters which allowed scripters to control HC's menubar when a movie played. The new version ALWAYS hides the menubar, and it will remain hidden until the command playMovie movieClose completely dumps the player from RAM. This can be VERY distracting if you have designed an 'application' with your own menus!

title: Version Control 2.1.SIT
description: Two external commands are attached to this stack. They give the stack developer a way to attach version numbers and comments to a stack in a 'vers' resource (like the resource that is attached to the HyperCard application). A Macintosh 'vers' resource stores 4 items: the version number in BCD form, the version number in ascii (text), a version comment, and a number indicating the country the file is localized for. If you "Get Info" on a file in Finder 6.1 or later, the version comment is the text that is displayed. The other data is available to any Mac program that opens the resource. You can use the ReadVersion XFCN to read the version number, the version comment, and the country code. It returns them as comma-separated items in a text string. (The comment will be enclosed in quotes.) You need to be in the stack in order to read its 'vers' data, so go there first if you're not. ReadVersion will return an error message if anything goes wrong. To change your stack's 'vers' resource, use the WriteVersion XCMD. It takes 2 or 3 parameters: the version number, the version comment and, optionally, the country code. It will update the 'vers' resource of the current stack, (or create one if none existed). Check 'the result' for error messages after calling WriteVersion.
modernAnalog: application build metadata editor/reader for version strings, release notes, and locale tags
coreUserJob: set and retrieve version metadata on a software artifact so builds can be identified and described consistently
whyRelevant: The card describes a durable release-management job: writing and reading structured version metadata, comments, and localization codes attached to an artifact. That maps cleanly to modern build pipelines, package metadata, app manifest versioning, and release labeling workflows.

title: Color Pictures
description: An XCMD to show PICT files or resources in HyperCard's window. As well as a bonus set of scripts to make pseudo picture objects. Way back in 1989 I had a color macintosh and HyperCard. No one was doing color for HyperCard so I took it apon myself to put something together. At about the same time Stan Gilbert came out with TKShowPict. His method was quite a bit better than my own so I stole it. What made his idea so important was NOT how to get at picture on the screen. That part was easy, but how to tell if it had been erased. While I will cover this in the background scripts and the script of the card button on the next card I did want to make sure credit was given where credit was due. Explore the scripts to get a good feel on how to fool folks into thinking HC really does color. Why not use his XCMD? Well Stan Gilberts was what I needed initally it had a two flaws: 1) I could not define a rectangle in which to place the picture. 2) I could not read in a PICT file. I needed to do both. So in the great tradition of necessity birthing invention. I took my ideas and merged them with his idea and came up with my own XCMD. Synatax ShowPicture [/], [/,[/],[Use Picture Rect ] Looks like ShowPicture 'Logo','0,0' Draw the PICT resource at card location 0,0. ShowPicture 'Bogus HD:Some Folder:My Picture', rect of cd btn 3, file Draw PICT file 'My Picture' at 'Bogus HD:Some Folder' into the rect of cd btn 3. ShowPicture 'Logo', rect of btn 2, True Draws PICT resource Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, File, True and ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, True, File Draws PICT File Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. Software and Hardware Reqirements HyperCard version 2.0 and system 4.0 or greater. If there is great enough outcry I can re-rev it so that it will work with HC 1.2.x. Justification The ShowPicture XCMD places a great deal of the burden on HyperTalk programmer to 'know' the state of the HC window at any given time. Is this fair? I am not sure. It works fine for me and a few other people I know who have used this XCMD. It is very fast, the code is only 2000 bytes. Most of the code is used for parsing the XCMD arguments (and as such most is skipped). The only other way to keep track of when HC redraws it's window is to patch a trap or two. This has been done by the BungDabba folks. And if you want to worry less about when HC redraws over your color pictures their product, which is free, may interest you. If you want blazing speed this will do it for you. Even though it will look kinda tacky sometimes.

title: CMU Tunnels
description: Newsgroups: alt.college.tunnels Subject: CMU Tunnels Hypercard stack (long) Date: 14 March 1994 Folks, I found something lying around on one of my old Mac disks that may interest some of you: an interactive hypercard map of the CMU steam tunnels. I wrote this stack back in '88 as part of a student application fair called StraightTalk. It's very simple, basically a map with hot spots that take you to pages describing the entrance or tunnel you clicked on. It was intended to demonstrate how you could do neat things with a Mac in a short period of time. It's not up to date, but I thought I would post it just for fun. Those of you who haven't actually been in tunnels can get a sense of how they are sometimes laid out, and I thought ex-CMU people might get kick out of reminiscing about old times. For current CMU people, you'll note the conspicuous absence of Resnik Hall & West Wing, the Parking Garage, and anything else that happened after 1988. Don't take this stack as an authoritative reference, but as a peek into the mid/late 80's CMU culture. The StraightTalk fair was officially sponsored, and no, I didn't get into any trouble for presenting this stack. I thought I would, and held my breath when a couple of Security folks came over, but nothing happened. Apple even had a photographer take pictures of all the presenters, including some of me near a tunnel entrance! I never saw the pictures, though... :( Anyway, here's the stack (called "Tunnels"). Remove everything above the "this file must be converted with binhex 4.0" line and then run it through binhex. Feel free to pass this on to other folks, put it on servers, ftp sites, etc. If you do, please send me a note letting me know so I can have a feel for its distribution. Ideally, I'd like it if the text portion of this message was included as a readme file so new folks would know some of the history. Enjoy, Dennis

description: Like Alice in Wonderland , you are a child jumping into a magical world filled with fantastical places and peculiar characters. Exploring the completely open world, you can listen to and have educational conversations with Mr. Rabbit, Mr. Dragon, and others. The entire world is accessible from the start. The original Macintosh version consists of six black-and-white stacks for the Macintosh's HyperCard software. Ports to other systems added color graphics. Also, the Macintosh floppy and CD versions are the same except for the cd version adding audio enhancements like voice-overs for the dialogue. I actually have no clue what the story is about, as I have a natural tendency to just spam the enter key to skip through the introduction. I played this game for the first time in 1996, and it stayed on my mind rent free ever since, so it shouldn't be too bad for a teenager project. A full playthrough of the demo is actually quite simple (yeah, unfortunately this is just a demo, and my efforts to contact that Erick guy were in vain, as it seems like the mail adress he left in the files to provide him feedback in 95' doesn't work anymore 30 years later, for some reason). Select a class for your character. Either Babarian, Ninja or Paladin. The two last classes have the advantage to let you cast spells, such as Fireball for the Ninja. Smash that enter key to skip through the story. Go to the temple, say hi, and skip the text. The temple is where you heal and save (note that saving quits the game). Go to the shop, and buy the first weapon useable for your class. Equip that weapon (the name of the weapon must be shown in the character's stats on the left of the screen, in the bottom, or it's not actively equipped). Go back to the town and head to the tunnels. Indeed, you can go to the tavern, but aside from a guy that tells you to go to the tunnels, and the bartender offering you free beverages to get you drunk (which doesn't work though, your character is too much of a badass unfortunately), there isn't anything to do. If you decided to buy a weapon an have it equiped, skip to 7. If you forgot to equip it or just skipped 4, perish on your first monster. You shouldn't have gone bare handed. You get ressurected with 1 HP. Go to the temple and heal before going back. Travel through the dungeon and have fun. The game is heavily based on RNG, and any hit, either from the character or a mob can range anywhere from 1 to 200 damage. In other words, while the game is not hard, it is very easy to die miserably on a weak enemy, even with the best gear, just because of bad luck. Just avoid getting there bare handed, and you will mostly be fine. The demo dungeon only provides two levels, which will roughtly take you around 2 hours, and when you beat the boss at the end of the second level, all the monsters just stop spawning. You just got your hands on a pretty great treasure and potions, but all of this is useless, since you won't encounter a single monster in this playthrough anymore. Why? Good question. I told you I spent my time skipping the story so, I don't know. The only thing you can do now is either start another playthrough with another class, or delete the demo. I would've adored a full game out of this, but I've yet to find the Erick guy. I'll keep you posted.

title: Flowers of Crystal
description: Flowers of Crystal is an educational game from the author of Granny's Garden. The game was designed to provide for creative activities in the classroom and is built around puzzle-solving. It consists of three parts: an introductory story and an adventure in two parts. A password from the first part is required to start the second. The introductory story is also included recorded on an audio tape as well as printed in a booklet. The game takes place on the planet Crystal which once had vast expanses of colourful flowers that are now thought to be extinct. The player is a visitor from Earth who explores the planet in an attempt to find the last remaining flower. In the first part, the player has to find four objects (special water, gold pot, fertiliser and soil). The gameplay involves travelling between seven locations (centre, city, factory, forest, lake, desert and mountains) and choosing which items to carry. The game starts out at the centre where the player chooses four items from three different menus. Items are needed when visiting some of the locations or give some protection when encountering hazards or some of the world's hostile creatures. The second part involves finding six parts of a flower dream to recreate a complete flower picture and then go to a castle. To progress it is necessary to obtain spells and use them at the right location.
